main_summary.summary: Reverend Joseph Echols Lowery, SCLC co-founder and longtime civil rights leader, was interviewed by Joe Mosnier on June 6, 2011, at Cascade United Methodist Church in Atlanta, Georgia, for the Civil Rights History Project. Lowery describes his pastorate at Warren Street Methodist Church in Mobile, Alabama, beginning in 1952, his early work with the NAACP under John LeFlore, and the Mobile bus desegregation ride on the Prichard route alongside Reverend S.M. McCree. He recounts meeting with C.K. Steele in Tallahassee, Kelly Miller Smith in Nashville, T.J. Jemison, and Fred Shuttlesworth to organize a South-wide movement, the interrupted Atlanta meeting after Ralph Abernathy's church was bombed, and SCLC's founding in New Orleans. He details the New York Times v. Sullivan libel suit, the seizure and auction of his car, the role of attorney M. Roland Nachman, and the Supreme Court reversal. Lowery describes the Selma campaign, Jimmie Lee Jackson's death in Marion, Bloody Sunday, the march to Montgomery, leading the committee that confronted Governor George Wallace, the Voting Rights Act of 1965, and delivering the benediction at Barack Obama's inauguration.
key_themes: Mobile bus desegregation and Prichard route
New York Times v. Sullivan libel suit
Selma to Montgomery march and Voting Rights Act
main_summary.historical_significance:
Lowery is a primary source on the founding moments of SCLC, including the interrupted Atlanta organizing meeting and the relocation of the founding convention to New Orleans after the bombing of Ralph Abernathy's church. As one of the four named defendants in the Sullivan libel case alongside Abernathy, Shuttlesworth, and S.S. Seay, he provides direct testimony on the property-seizure phase that preceded the Supreme Court reversal, including the auction of his confiscated car and its purchase by R.B. DeWitt of the Mobile YMCA.

The interview also documents Lowery's role at the climax of the Selma campaign as chairman of the committee appointed by Dr. King to present the march's demands to Governor George Wallace, and his account of the confrontation at the Alabama State Capitol with state troopers and the National Guard. His recollections of being an early civil-rights-leadership supporter of Barack Obama, traveling to Selma in 2007 for the Brown Chapel AME event where Obama claimed the Joshua-generation mantle, and giving the benediction at the 2009 inauguration anchor the throughline from Montgomery to the first Black presidency.

chapters.title: Warren Street and the NAACP
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier at Cascade United Methodist Church in Atlanta in June 2011, Joseph Lowery opens on coming to Warren Street Church in Mobile in 1952, where the NAACP held its Sunday afternoon meetings, and how he jumped in on voter registration and education before SCLC existed.

chapters.title: A Racism Less Toxic
chapters.summary: Lowery describes John LeFlore, the Mobile NAACP leader whose lawsuit-and-complaint style differed from his own activism and whose friendship later cooled, and a city he calls a little New Orleans, racist but not with the toxic racism of Birmingham or Montgomery, where the Catholic commissioner Joe Langan offered a warmer hand.

chapters.title: A Paper Sack Full of Money
chapters.summary: As president of the Interdenominational Ministerial Alliance, Lowery recounts carrying the first thousand dollars of outside support from a Mobile church to the Montgomery Improvement Association, the crowd going wild at the paper sack of money, and then launching Mobile's own bus movement with Fellowship of Reconciliation workshops on the dangerous Prichard route.

chapters.title: Please Sit Down, the Bus Is Moving
chapters.summary: Lowery tells of riding the front seat to Prichard with his Baptist partner, a drunk man with a bottle who ordered the driver to make them move, and how he defused it nonviolently by warning the man it was dangerous to stand on a moving bus, the man finally storming off, and a Black rider at the end of the line unsure how she felt about the change.

chapters.title: The NAACP Is Outlawed
chapters.summary: After a pause, Lowery describes the 1955 to 1956 boycott months, Governor Patterson outlawing the NAACP and demanding its membership roll while the group refused and was held in contempt, the Montgomery Improvement Association rising in its place under King and Abernathy, and King telling the crowd after his house was bombed to seek no eye for an eye.

chapters.title: Born in New Orleans
chapters.summary: Lowery recalls leaders including Fred Shuttlesworth, C.K. Steele, Kelly Miller Smith, and T.J. Jemison gathering in Atlanta to build a South-wide movement, the meeting cut short when Abernathy's church was bombed and reconvened in New Orleans as SCLC's official birthplace, a Klan-seized Montgomery night spent hiding in the Ben Moore Hotel, and King elected president with Lowery an officer.

chapters.title: He Didn't Charge Us Anything
chapters.summary: Lowery remembers Bayard Rustin coming to Montgomery to help draft the documents that formed the basis of SCLC without charging a fee, and King's emergence leading the first boycott a city of fifty thousand Black people sustained for over a year, accepted by many out of confidence in his leadership and making him the natural president when SCLC organized.

chapters.title: The First Woman to Lead
chapters.summary: Lowery credits Ella Baker as SCLC's first full-time executive director after John Tilley, a strong woman whom history may have overlooked as the first to give that executive leadership to the movement, who did not always get along with the preachers but whose inspiration helped organize the students into SNCC.

chapters.title: We Were Outmanned
chapters.summary: Lowery lays out the two roots of nonviolence: the practical reality of a small army with no weapons or uniforms that could never have won a violent confrontation against the National Guard and troopers, and the theological reality that the most independent leader was the preacher, accountable to his congregation, drawing on the example of Jesus forgiving from the cross.

chapters.title: We Became New Creatures
chapters.summary: Lowery reflects that nonviolence worked inwardly as well as outwardly, freeing them from hatred, recalling George Wallace astounded by their spirit, the citizens' council unable to understand a courage they tried to call cowardice, and a Nashville white woman who opposed the direct action movement until she watched their conduct and said the spirit of the Lord was upon them.

chapters.title: The Lord Spoke and I Said Madison
chapters.summary: Returning after a break, Lowery describes the late 1950s libel campaign and the seizure of his car under a judgment, his home safe because he lived in the church parsonage, and how, having warned his lawyer not to mention the Huntsville property he inherited from his father, he answered on the stand that he was born in Madison rather than Huntsville, sparing the land taken from Abernathy.

chapters.title: Sitting With Reinhold Niebuhr
chapters.topic: The Libel Suit and Its Reversal
chapters.summary: Lowery recounts the libel suit argued to the Supreme Court with the New York Times and four ministers, Abernathy, Shuttlesworth, Seay, and himself, the Times refusing to shelter them under its bond, the city's lawyer Roland Nachman invoking Sammy Davis Jr. and his marriage to a white actress to inflame the jury, and the eventual reversal that returned the car his Methodist members had bought back at auction for his wife.

chapters.title: Over My Dead Body
chapters.summary: Lowery turns to the students entering in 1960 and the movement growing from buses and voter registration to segregation as a whole, the 1964 ruling against segregation in public accommodations, and demonstrating at Morrison's cafeterias in Nashville until the chain's president vowed to desegregate over his dead body, then desegregated in July and died a month later.

chapters.title: The Era of Self-Determination
chapters.summary: Lowery names the Montgomery boycott as the beginning of an era of self-determination, the first time an entire community refused the back of the bus regardless of court or legislature, and the sit-ins as another expression of the same spirit, refusing to buy toothpaste where they could not eat lunch, for which he later received an award from Greensboro.

chapters.part: Selma and the March to Montgomery
chapters.title: We Did Not Hate Bull Connor
chapters.summary: Lowery describes how the spirit of nonviolence spared them from hating even Bull Connor or King's killer James Earl Ray, and how after Birmingham they retreated to the A.G. Gaston Motel and chose Selma for a voting campaign over President Johnson's plea to slow down after the 1964 act, picking the black belt county where SNCC had already been persecuted.

chapters.part: Selma and the March to Montgomery
chapters.title: How Long, Not Long
chapters.summary: Lowery recounts Dallas County with its handful of Black registered voters, the march conceived after Jimmie Lee Jackson was killed in Marion and mourners marched to his grave, his wife's memorials to Jackson and James Reeb, Bloody Sunday halted at the bridge, and the eventual arrival in Montgomery where King declared how long, not long.

chapters.part: Selma and the March to Montgomery
chapters.title: The Blue Sea Parted
chapters.summary: Named by King to chair the committee carrying the march's demands to Governor Wallace, Lowery tells of state troopers lining the Capitol steps like a blue sea until the National Guard general ordered them aside with bayonets, his refusal to hand the petition to a secretary, the later ninety-minute meeting where Wallace shredded scraps of paper, and the Voting Rights Act of 1965 that changed the nation's voting pattern.

chapters.part: A Black President and the Benediction
chapters.title: There'd Be a Black President
chapters.summary: Lowery reflects on hearing Bobby Kennedy in the Freedom Riders film predict a Black president none of them thought they would live to see, and how, feeling God moving in history, he became an early supporter of Barack Obama when few civil rights leaders were, unlike the purely symbolic Jesse Jackson campaign of 1984.

chapters.part: A Black President and the Benediction
chapters.title: Good Crazy
chapters.summary: Lowery describes going to Brown Chapel AME in Selma in 2007 to hear Obama, his good crazy speech that YouTube spread across the country, Obama naming himself the Joshua generation to Lowery and John Lewis's Moses generation, marching the bridge together with his wife, and later praying at a Georgia Tech rally and campaigning as far as a chilly Iowa where strangers held yes we can signs.

chapters.part: A Black President and the Benediction
chapters.title: The Last Word at the Inauguration
chapters.summary: Lowery recalls Obama calling his cell phone to ask him to give the inaugural benediction, choosing the benediction for its last word, and standing on the steps with hazed-over eyes near eighty hearing King's summons of forty years before answered in the election, weeping as the anthem sounded better though nothing had changed but the country, before closing with thanks and the Joseph Lowery Institute.

loc_video.caption: Joseph Echols Lowery oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Atlanta, Georgia, 2011 June 06
